narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 65% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic indicators like test scores, grades, and background factors, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. In your case, the three biggest influences were your LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, and your Year 3 decile ranking in school. Most significantly, your LSAT score of 21.30—well below the average of 37.27 for students who passed the bar—strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting failure, as lower scores like yours have historically correlated with tougher outcomes on the exam. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, close to the 3.24 average among bar passers, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward success. However, your Year 3 decile of 2—much lower than the 5.91 average for those who passed—counteracted that by signaling a concerning dip in performance, further tilting the model toward failure. Compared to typical admitted students, who often enter full-time programs with stronger Year 3 standings and higher LSATs, your profile stood out as riskier despite a solid undergrad record and middle-income family background that aligns with many successful applicants. Other elements, like your first-year decile of 6 being right around the passing average, played smaller roles overall. This isn't a judgment on your potential or drive—it's the model highlighting where your academics most deviated from proven paths to bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, as it's the top factor here and could dramatically shift future predictions. Strengthening your recent grades or seeking targeted bar prep could also help, and reapplying after building those credentials often leads to better outcomes for students in similar spots. Many have turned predictions like this around with focused effort—you can too.
